title: NULLIF Function
content:
### NULLIF Function

NULLIF compares two expressions and returns NULL if they are equal, otherwise returns the first expression.

---

### Basic NULLIF Syntax

```sql
SELECT NULLIF(salary, 0) AS salary_or_null
FROM employees;

-- Returns NULL if salary is 0, otherwise returns salary
```

### Common Use Cases

```sql
-- Prevent division by zero
SELECT name,
       total_sales / NULLIF(num_orders, 0) AS avg_order_value
FROM sales_summary;

-- Treat empty strings as NULL
SELECT NULLIF(TRIM(notes), '') AS clean_notes
FROM orders;
```

### NULLIF with Aggregation

```sql
-- Exclude zeros from average calculation
SELECT AVG(NULLIF(salary, 0)) AS avg_nonzero_salary
FROM employees;

-- Count only non-empty categories
SELECT COUNT(NULLIF(category, '')) AS non_empty_categories
FROM products;
```

### NULLIF vs COALESCE

| Function | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| NULLIF(a, b) | Returns NULL if a equals b |
| COALESCE(a, b) | Returns first non-NULL value |

```sql
-- These are complementary:
-- COALESCE fills in defaults, NULLIF replaces specific values with NULL
```

> **Key Point:** NULLIF is the inverse of COALESCE in a sense — it creates NULL values where a specific condition is met, while COALESCE replaces NULL values with a default.
codeExample:
SELECT department,
       SUM(salary) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) AS avg_salary_per_employee
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;
